title: The 'Purple Cow' Strategy: How to Stand Out on YouTube

transcript.text: So, something is happening on YouTube right now, and most creators haven't even noticed yet, which gives you a distinct advantage. An unknown student got 400,000 views by introducing his Italian poetry professor to Kendrick Lamar. A chef is growing their channel by over 4 million views by making depression meals from the 1930s. And this guy's building a YouTube channel while trying to beat every single N64 game ever made. It's been over 4 years and he's still going with over 147,000 subscribers following the journey. It's a tactic quietly dominating YouTube, and it's called the Purple Cow. In 2003, a marketer named Seth Goden was driving through France with his family. And on that drive, they were looking at cows. A lot of cows. After a while, every cow looked the same. But then he had a thought that became the most cited business book for the next 20 years. What if one of those cows happened to be purple? You would stop the car, you'd take a picture, you'd text it to your friends, not because purple cows are better than brown cows, but because a purple cow would be remarkable. And this whole concept rests on one detail inside that word. Remarkable Aable. Think about the last time you sent somebody a YouTube link with no context whatsoever because the video spoke for itself. That right there is the bar. And by the end of this video, you will know how to clear it. Our data shows that there are over 217,000 active English-speaking fitness channels on this platform. As you can imagine, all of them are making similar videos to one another. But then you have this creator who decided to do the One Punch Man anime workout. 100 push-ups, 100 sit-ups, 100 squats, and a 10K run every single day on camera. The video went viral and inspired an entire new subgenre of fitness content. Then you have this creator who's asking which NFL defense could have held the Persians at thermopoly. In his own words, he's making sports history content, a video category I believe I invented. And we already mentioned the beast 721 who's beating every N64 game ever made, all 394 of them. And we know this because of the counter that sits on the top of every single video. All of these channels pass the test. You could describe any one of them in one sentence and the person you're describing them to will almost immediately want to go and learn more. That is a purple cow. Most creators when they get serious about growing on YouTube go about it the completely wrong way. First, they find the biggest channel in their niche and they'll do things like reverse engineer the thumbnails, copy the title structure one for one, and they make what they think is like a slightly better version of the video they just watched. Think about it from this perspective. If YouTube offered a viewer your video and a video about the same thing from a more established channel, usually that more established channel is going to win pretty much every single time. They simply have more videos, more social proof, more views on that type of video. The opportunity cost for that viewer from switching from the established creator to you is relatively high, and they get very little reward. In fact, it's hardly noticeable because the person they're switching to is basically just doing the same thing. In that scenario, you just become another brown cow. You don't want to be better. Better is invisible. What you want to be is different. That is when people notice. The creators we just looked at, none of them are doing better. They're doing different in a very specific and structured way. Once you see the patterns, you can build your own version for your channel. One quick note, Seth Goden did coin the concept of purple cow, but he never really categorized the types. He just gave us the criteria of remarkable and then left us to figure out the rest. So, what we did is we pulled hundreds of breakout channels and we watched for patterns to surface. Four of them continued to come up over and over again. The first is called format collision, and that's what happens when you take two completely unrelated world domains and you smash them together as hard as you can until they stick to each other. A good example would be the One Punch Man workout creator we looked at earlier. That is textbook format collision. Two domains that exist completely independently from one another. On one side, an anime fandom and on the other fitness transformation content, but Purple Cow is the collision of those two things. You take the cartoon workout and you just do it. And the premise itself is your reason to share this content. Sometimes the more wild the mashup, the more remarkable the results. Take a look here. We have ASMR gun sounds. Yes, this is a real thing. And it may sound ridiculous, but this creator's channel got 57 million views from it. ASMR is very often soft, soothing sounds to relax a viewer, maybe lull them off to sleep. And firearms are loud and aggressive. So, you would think these two very exact opposite things wouldn't work. But that juxtaposition is what makes you go, "Wait, what?" and want to know more and potentially share it with other people. The second type of purple cow we see on YouTube is called alternative context. And it works a bit differently. Instead of pairing to real things, you take a real topic and you pair it with a fully realized imagined frame. A frame with its own internal logic that the viewer then steps into. Tier Zoo is one of the best examples of the strategy. Topic biology frame Earth is an MMO. He doesn't say things like humans adapted to their environment. He says humans specked into the cooperative loot sharing play style that's dominant in the current meta. Survival Kitchen 1930 is a cooking channel that lives entirely inside its alternative context. They approach the topic of cooking through the frame of the Great Depression. The persona, the era, the urgency. Every title is around a poverty and survival framework. When shelves go empty, this food will save you. 2 years of food for pennies. Docu Dubrey does the same thing for a completely different audience. He takes the topics of modern professional sports and frames them through ancient history. That unique way of thinking and presenting a subject is what makes viewers click and share with their friends. What ties these three channels together is that alternative context is the point of their videos. Tier Zoo without the gaming lens is just zoology and survival kitchen without the 1930s persona is just shelf stable recipes. The different lens is doing all the work for you and turning an ordinary channel into a purple cow. Then we have the commitment premise. Commitment is the most defensible purple cow you can build. A finite project so audacious that the project itself is the marketing. Nobody can copy you because in order to keep up with you, they would need to spend the same months or years you've already put in to catch up. The Beast 721 is beating every N64 game ever made.
